title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Struphy is …

… an open-source Python package for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), primarily designed for plasma physics — though not limited to it.



💯% Python — Write models in clean, readable Python.




💥 Pyccel-powered kernels — High-performance Fortran code generated via Pyccel.




💻 Simple API — Run complex plasma simulations with minimal boilerplate.




🌐 Open-source & community-driven — Built for collaboration and accessibility.

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Numerical methods

Particle-in-cell (PIC) method for kinetic models:
\[
f(t, x, v) = \sum_k w_k\, \delta(x - x_k(t))\, \delta(v - v_k(t))
\]


Finite element exterior calculus (FEEC) for fluid/field variables (via Psydac):
\[
\mathbf E(t, x) = \sum_i e_i(t)\, \mathbf \Lambda^1_i(x)\,, \qquad \mathbf B(t, x) = \sum_i b_i(t)\, \mathbf \Lambda^2_i(x)
\]


Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH) for fluid models:
\[
n(t, x) = \sum_k w_k\, W(x - x_k(t))\,,\qquad \mathbf u(t, x) = \sum_k \frac{w_k}{n(t, x_k(t))}\, \mathbf v_k(t)\, W(x - x_k(t))
\]


Struphy provides MPI/OpenMP data structures for these methods (GPU via cuda-python in the second half of 2026 - ACH project Max Lindqvist)


\[
\phantom{}
\]
Under consideration: Hermite polynomials for velocity discretization (C. Negulescu)

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Purpose of Struphy


Provide a common Python API for researchers working on plasma physics PDEs




(such a thing exists for quantum physics, check out QuTiP).



Care about maintainable code, avoid “technical debt” in research software




Instead of losing research code, provide a framework for its preservation and reuse.




Enable computational plasma research for a wider audience, including students and researchers that are not experts in programming.

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Installation Options

Install on bare metal (Linux/macOS)

From PyPI (recommended)
From source (for development)

Use Docker (cross-platform)
Run tutorials on Binder (cloud-based)


🌐 Repo: github.com/struphy-hub/struphy


🌐 Docs: struphy-hub.github.io/struphy


🌐 Tutorials: github.com/struphy-hub/struphy-tutorials

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Install on bare metal (Linux/macOS)


Use a virtual environment:

python -m venv struphy-env
source struphy-env/bin/activate 



From PyPI (with physics packages):

pip install -U struphy[phys]



From source (for development):

git clone https://github.com/struphy-hub/struphy.git
cd struphy
pip install -e .[phys,dev]



Compile kernels (Fortran code generated via Pyccel):

struphy compile

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Use Docker (cross-platform)


Pull the latest image from Docker Hub and run it:

docker pull spossann/struphy:main
docker run -it --rm spossann/struphy:main



List the existing environments and activate one:

ls struphy_*
source struphy_fortran_/env_fortran_/bin/activate

Check the compilation:

struphy compile --status

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Run tutorials on Binder (cloud-based)

🌐 Tutorials: github.com/struphy-hub/struphy-tutorials






We will run through the first few tutorials together, but feel free to explore the rest on your own time!

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Models (PDEs)

from struphy import models
models.__all__

['ColdPlasma',

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Model Info

from struphy.models import Maxwell
Maxwell.info()

Maxwell's equations in vacuum for electromagnetic field evolution.


To see detailed information on the model, run the following methods:

Maxwell.pde()
Maxwell.normalization()
Maxwell.scalar_quantities()
Maxwell.discretization()
Maxwell.long_description()
Maxwell.examples()
Maxwell.use_cases()
Maxwell.cannot_be_used_for()

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Create a Simulation

from struphy import Simulation

model = Maxwell()
sim = Simulation(model=model, verbose=True)

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Run

sim.run(verbose=True)

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Time, grid, and Derham options

from struphy import Time, grids, DerhamOptions

time_opts = Time(dt=0.1, Tend=0.3)

grid = grids.TensorProductGrid(num_elements=(32, 1, 1))

derham_opts = DerhamOptions(degree=(3, 1, 1))


For more info on available options, see the API guide in the Struphy docs:

🌐 Docs: struphy-hub.github.io/struphy

A good way to explore the options is to use Pylance in your code editor (e.g., VSCode) to inspect the available attributes and their docstrings.

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
section: New model object with initial conditions
text:
New model object with initial conditions

model = Maxwell()
print(model)

Maxwell
    em_fields:
        e_field:    FEECVariable (Hcurl)
        b_field:    FEECVariable (Hdiv)





from struphy import perturbations

fun1 = perturbations.ModesCos(ls=(1,), amps=(1e-1,), comp=1)
fun2 = perturbations.ModesSin(ls=(2,), amps=(1e-2,), comp=2)

model.em_fields.e_field.add_perturbation(perturbation=fun1)
model.em_fields.e_field.add_perturbation(perturbation=fun2)



Check what the perturbations look like:

from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

x = np.linspace(0, 1, 100)

# Plot 1: First component in x-direction
axes[0].plot(fun1(x, 0.5, 0.5))
axes[0].set_title("fun1 (E-field y-component)")
axes[0].set_xlabel("x")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Value")

# Plot 2: Add your second plot
axes[1].plot(fun2(x, 0.5, 0.5))
axes[1].set_title("fun2 (E-field z-component)")
axes[1].set_xlabel("x")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Value")

plt.tight_layout()

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Create a new simulation object
The following simulation will inherit all the initial conditions and parameters from sim, except for the ones we explicitly override.

sim2 = Simulation(
    model=model,
    time_opts=time_opts,
    grid=grid,
    derham_opts=derham_opts,
    verbose=True,
)

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
You can compare sim objects

sim == sim2

False



We can serialze sims through dicts:

sim3 = Simulation.from_dict(sim.to_dict())

sim == sim3

True




This is the same as

sim4 = sim.spawn_sister()

sim == sim4

True




Only in devel so far - not yet in main:

sim.export("sim.yaml")
sim5 = Simulation.from_file("sim.yaml")
assert sim == sim5

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Run and post process
Run:

sim2.run()


Use the pproc method for flexibility in post-processing and visualization:

sim2.pproc()

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Load plotting data

sim2.load_plotting_data()

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Inspect data types

e_field = sim2.spline_values.em_fields.e_field_log
print(e_field)

type(self.data) = <class 'dict'>
len(self.data) = 4
key = np.float64(0.0)    shape = [(33, 2, 2), (33, 2, 2), (33, 2, 2)]
key = np.float64(0.1)    shape = [(33, 2, 2), (33, 2, 2), (33, 2, 2)]
key = np.float64(0.2)    shape = [(33, 2, 2), (33, 2, 2), (33, 2, 2)]
key = np.float64(0.3)    shape = [(33, 2, 2), (33, 2, 2), (33, 2, 2)]

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Plot snapshots

fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 3, figsize=(15, 12))

for n, (time, data) in enumerate(e_field.data.items()):
    
  # Plot 1: First component in x-direction
  axes[n, 0].plot(data[0][:, 0, 0])
  axes[n, 0].set_title(f"E_1 (x-direction) at time {time}")
  axes[n, 0].set_xlabel("x")
  axes[n, 0].set_ylabel("Value")
  axes[n, 0].set_ylim(-1.5e-1, 1.5e-1)  # Set y-limits for better comparison across time steps

  # Plot 2: Add your second plot
  axes[n, 1].plot(data[1][:, 0, 0])
  axes[n, 1].set_title(f"E_2 (x-direction) at time {time} ")
  axes[n, 1].set_xlabel("x")
  axes[n, 1].set_ylabel("Value")
  axes[n, 1].set_ylim(-1.5e-1, 1.5e-1)  # Set y-limits for better comparison across time steps
  # Plot 3: Add your third plot
  axes[n, 2].plot(data[2][:, 0, 0])
  axes[n, 2].set_title(f"E_3 (x-direction) at time {time}")
  axes[n, 2].set_xlabel("x")
  axes[n, 2].set_ylabel("Value")
  axes[n, 2].set_ylim(-1.5e-1, 1.5e-1)

plt.tight_layout()

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Default launch files from CLI
struphy params MODEL
This will create a file params_MODEL.py in the current working directory.

To run the model type
python params_MODEL.py


For parallel runs, use
mpirun -n N python params_MODEL.py
or copy the command to a job script for your cluster’s scheduler (e.g., SLURM, PBS).

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Example: Vlasov-Ampère model

from struphy.models import VlasovAmpereOneSpecies
VlasovAmpereOneSpecies.info()

Vlasov-Ampère system for a single kinetic species in an electric field.


To see detailed information on the model, run the following methods:

VlasovAmpereOneSpecies.pde()
VlasovAmpereOneSpecies.normalization()
VlasovAmpereOneSpecies.scalar_quantities()
VlasovAmpereOneSpecies.discretization()
VlasovAmpereOneSpecies.long_description()
VlasovAmpereOneSpecies.examples()
VlasovAmpereOneSpecies.use_cases()
VlasovAmpereOneSpecies.cannot_be_used_for()

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Launch file API
struphy params VlasovAmpereOneSpecies
cat params_VlasovAmpereOneSpecies.py 
Or inspect in your favorite code editor, for live inspection and editing:

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Example: LinearMHD

from struphy.models import LinearMHD
LinearMHD.info()

from struphy import domains, equils

Linear ideal MHD with zero-flow equilibrium for magnetohydrodynamic wave propagation.


To see detailed information on the model, run the following methods:

LinearMHD.pde()
LinearMHD.normalization()
LinearMHD.scalar_quantities()
LinearMHD.discretization()
LinearMHD.long_description()
LinearMHD.examples()
LinearMHD.use_cases()
LinearMHD.cannot_be_used_for()

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Example: LinearMHD (ctd.)

model = LinearMHD()
domain = domains.DESCunit()
equil = equils.DESCequilibrium(use_nfp=False)

sim = Simulation(
    model=model,
    domain=domain,
    equil=equil,
)

sim.show_domain(scalars="absB0", window_size = (850, 250), zoom_factor=2.0)

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Example files
Visit the repo for example parameter files:

🌐 Repo: github.com/struphy-hub/struphy-parameter-files

Easy to launch and post-process examples:
cd VlasovAmpereOneSpecies
cd two_stream
python params_two_stream.py
python pproc_two_stream.py

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Benchmarks
In the making …

Current work focuses on benchmarking against other HPC plasma codes such as GEMPICX, GENE, ORB5, and JOREK, as well as against analytical solutions for linear instabilities and nonlinear dynamics.

title: Struphy 3.1  Structure-Preserving  Hybrid Code
text:
Plasma HPC framework in Python

Struphy 3.0: A structure-preserving hybrid code for plasma simulations in Python.
Combines particle and grid methods with high-performance kernels (Pyccel).
Easy-to-use API for setting up and running complex plasma simulations.
You can integrate your model into the API.
Open-source and community-driven development - get in touch!
QR code to the slides below - Thank you!
